schools.summary: Chrysostom: holy indeed are all the epistles of Paul, but some advantage have those he sent after he was in bonds — those to the Ephesians and Philemon, that to Timothy, that to the Philippians, and the one before us, for this also was sent when he was a prisoner (as he writes, 'for which I am also in bonds,' Colossians 4:3); Paul opening the letter as an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, joining Timothy as a brother — the apostolic greeting grounded in God's will (Paul, beginning this prison epistle, naming his office and its source: an apostle of Jesus Christ not by his own seeking or man's appointment but 'by the will of God,' and associating Timothy with himself as 'our brother,' so the authority behind the letter is divine and the affection in it joined to a beloved fellow-worker).

themes: Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ
by the will of God
and Timotheus our brother
the apostolic salutation

schools.summary: Gill: the persons addressed are described as 'saints,' holy men — not by birth (all being unholy and unclean by nature), nor by baptism (which neither takes away sin nor gives grace), nor merely externally by outward reformation, but by separation, being by an act of eternal election set apart for God and sanctified by the Spirit; Paul addressing the saints and faithful brethren in Christ at Colosse, with the benediction of grace and peace from the Father and the Lord Jesus — the holy people greeted with grace and peace (Paul naming the believers by what God's grace had made them, 'saints,' set apart and sanctified, and 'faithful brethren in Christ,' believing and steadfast members of one family, and invoking upon them the twin blessings of grace, God's free favour, and peace, its fruit, flowing jointly from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ).

themes: to the saints and faithful brethren in Christ which are at Colosse
grace be unto you, and peace
from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ
the holy people greeted with grace and peace

schools.summary: Gill: the epistle is introduced with a thanksgiving to God, to whom praise and thankfulness are always due as a Creator and preserver, as the author of all good things, as the Father of mercies temporal and spiritual, and as the covenant God and Father of his people through Christ; Matthew Henry notes Paul's thankful, prayerful spirit; Paul declaring that he and Timothy give thanks to God the Father of the Lord Jesus, praying always for them — the thankful, prayerful heart of the apostle (Paul opening, as is his custom, with thanksgiving to God, named 'the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ,' the fountain of all the grace shown the Colossians, and with the assurance that he prays for them always, so gratitude for what God has wrought in them is joined to unceasing intercession for their further good).

themes: we give thanks to God and the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ
praying always for you
the thankful, prayerful heart of the apostle
gratitude joined to constant intercession

schools.summary: Gill: their 'faith in Christ Jesus' means not only their hearty assent to the whole doctrine of faith concerning the person, offices, and grace of Christ, their soundness and steadfastness in it, and their sincere and constant profession of it, but the grace of faith itself wrought in their hearts; Paul giving the matter of his thanks — that he had heard of their faith in Christ Jesus and their love to all the saints — the twin graces of faith and love as the ground of thanks (Paul's thanksgiving resting on the good report that had reached him of two great Christian graces flourishing among the Colossians: their faith, fixed on Christ Jesus, both the grace of believing and steadfast holding of the truth, and their love, reaching out to all the saints, not a narrow or partial affection but a broad love embracing the whole household of faith, the very marks of a true and living Christianity).

themes: since we heard of your faith in Christ Jesus
the love which ye have to all the saints
the twin graces of faith and love
the ground of Paul's thanksgiving

schools.summary: Gill: the hope laid up in heaven is the rich treasure of glory and happiness reserved there, which encouraged the saints to believe in Christ and to hold fast the profession of their faith, and to love the saints, knowing the reward to come; Paul declaring that their faith and love sprang from the hope laid up for them in heaven, of which they had heard in the word of the truth of the gospel — the heavenly hope that nourishes the present graces (Paul rooting the Colossians' faith and love in their hope: the sure and certain expectation of the glory 'laid up,' treasured, reserved, kept safe, for them in heaven, which the gospel, 'the word of the truth,' had first made known to them, so the prospect of the coming inheritance feeds and sustains their believing and loving here below).

themes: the hope which is laid up for you in heaven
ye heard before in the word of the truth of the gospel
the heavenly hope that nourishes faith and love
the treasured glory the gospel made known

schools.summary: Gill: the gospel came to them from God, from heaven, from Christ, out of Jerusalem (from whence the word of the Lord was to come), by the ministers of the gospel who came to Colosse and preached it; a wonderful instance of the free grace of God, since they did not seek after it but it came to them (the Syriac rendering 'which is preached unto you'); Paul observing that the gospel which had come to them was, as in all the world, bringing forth fruit, as it had among them since the day they knew the grace of God in truth — the fruitful, world-wide gospel that had taken root in them (Paul commending the gospel as a living, growing thing: it had 'come' to the Colossians by God's free grace unsought, was spreading throughout the whole world, and everywhere, among them no less, was 'bringing forth fruit,' working real change and producing the graces of holiness, from the very day they had truly known the grace of God not in word only but in truth).

themes: which is come unto you, as it is in all the world
bringeth forth fruit, as it doth also in you
since the day ye... knew the grace of God in truth
the fruitful, world-wide gospel

schools.summary: Barnes: Epaphras was then with Paul (Philemon 1:23); he had probably been sent by the church at Colosse to consult Paul in reference to some matters pertaining to the church there, and it is evident he was a minister there; Paul commending Epaphras, their dear fellowservant and a faithful minister of Christ, from whom they had learned the gospel — the faithful pastor who had brought them the truth (Paul naming with honour the man through whom the Colossians had been taught: Epaphras, whom he calls his own 'dear fellowservant,' a beloved companion in the Lord's service, and 'a faithful minister of Christ' for them, so the gospel they had received was no corrupt or partial version but the true message faithfully delivered by a trustworthy servant of Christ on their behalf).

themes: as ye also learned of Epaphras our dear fellowservant
who is for you a faithful minister of Christ
the faithful pastor who brought them the truth
the true gospel faithfully delivered

schools.summary: Gill: Epaphras declared their love — not only their love to God and Christ and all the saints, but also their love to the apostle, though they had only heard of him and of his great capacity, faithfulness, and usefulness in preaching, which had endeared him to them; and this love, he says, was 'in the Spirit,' that is, spiritual love, to distinguish it from a natural or carnal affection; Paul stating that Epaphras had declared to him their love in the Spirit — the spiritual love reported by the faithful messenger (the same Epaphras who had taught the Colossians the gospel also bringing back to Paul the good news of their love, a love marked as 'in the Spirit,' wrought by the Holy Spirit, spiritual in its nature and source, not a mere natural fondness, so Paul knew of their genuine, Spirit-given affection even toward himself whom they had not seen).

themes: who also declared unto us your love
in the Spirit
the spiritual love reported by the messenger
Spirit-given love, not natural fondness

schools.summary: Chrysostom: as in the games men cheer on most those who are near upon gaining the victory, so Paul most exhorts those who have achieved the greater part; because he heard of their faith and love and has good hopes, he is hopeful to ask future blessings for them, and not for one day only but constantly ('since the day we heard it, we do not cease to pray'); Paul declaring that, since the day he heard of them, he had not ceased to pray that they be filled with the knowledge of God's will in all wisdom and spiritual understanding — the prayer for fulness of spiritual knowledge (Paul, prompted by the good report, praying unceasingly that the Colossians, already rich in faith and love, might grow yet richer in the knowledge of God's will, being 'filled,' completely supplied, with it, and that not a bare or notional knowledge but one joined to 'all wisdom and spiritual understanding,' a deep, Spirit-taught grasp of divine things issuing in holy living).

themes: since the day we heard it, do not cease to pray for you
filled with the knowledge of his will
in all wisdom and spiritual understanding
the prayer for fulness of spiritual knowledge

schools.summary: Gill: to 'walk worthy of the Lord' (the Lord Jesus Christ being designed, not 'God' as some versions read) is to walk by faith in him, after his Spirit, according to his word and in his ordinances, having a conversation as becomes his gospel and worthy of the calling wherewith the saints are called; Paul praying that they might walk worthy of the Lord unto all pleasing, fruitful in every good work, and increasing in the knowledge of God — the practical end of knowledge in a holy, fruitful life (Paul showing the purpose of the fuller knowledge he sought: that their lives might answer to it, walking 'worthy of the Lord' in a manner befitting his name and grace and 'unto all pleasing,' seeking in everything to please him, and proving the reality of that walk by being 'fruitful in every good work' and 'increasing in the knowledge of God,' so knowledge and life rise together).

themes: that ye might walk worthy of the Lord unto all pleasing
being fruitful in every good work
increasing in the knowledge of God
the practical end of knowledge in a holy life

schools.summary: Gill: this is still a continuation of the apostle's prayer; having prayed for an increase of spiritual knowledge and that it be put into practice (a worthy walk), Paul proceeds to pray for strength, that they might be enabled to practise what they had knowledge of, to walk worthily, to please God in all things, to bring forth fruit with patience, and to persevere; Paul praying that they be strengthened with all might, according to God's glorious power, unto all patience and longsuffering with joyfulness — the divine strengthening for joyful endurance (Paul praying for inward power: that the Colossians be 'strengthened with all might,' fully empowered, and 'according to his glorious power,' in proportion to God's own infinite strength not their weakness, the special end being endurance, 'all patience,' steadfastness under trial, and 'longsuffering,' forbearance under provocation, and that not grimly but 'with joyfulness,' bearing all with a glad and triumphant spirit).

themes: strengthened with all might, according to his glorious power
unto all patience and longsuffering
with joyfulness
the divine strengthening for joyful endurance

schools.summary: Gill: thanks are given to God the Father (as the Vulgate and Syriac read), who is both the Father of Christ by nature and of all his people by adoption; it was he who made the saints meet to be partakers of the heavenly glory, who delivers them from the power of sin and translates them into the kingdom of his dear Son; Paul giving thanks to the Father, who has made them meet to be partakers of the inheritance of the saints in light — the Father's grace in fitting his people for glory (Paul turning to thanksgiving, owning that it is the Father who has 'made us meet,' qualified, fitted, made capable, to share in 'the inheritance of the saints in light,' the glorious portion reserved for the holy ones in the realm of light and glory, so the very fitness for heaven is not the believer's own achievement but the Father's gracious work, for which thanks are due).

themes: giving thanks unto the Father
which hath made us meet to be partakers
the inheritance of the saints in light
fitness for heaven the Father's gracious work

schools.summary: Gill: the 'power of darkness' is the power of Satan, who, though once an angel of light, is now darkness itself and reserved in chains of darkness; he is the ruler of the darkness of this world, his kingdom a kingdom of darkness, and he blinds the minds of unbelievers, keeps them in darkness, and increases the natural darkness of their minds; Paul declaring that the Father has delivered them from the power of darkness and translated them into the kingdom of his dear Son — the rescue from Satan's realm into the kingdom of Christ (Paul magnifying the Father's grace by describing the change: they have been 'delivered,' rescued and snatched, from 'the power of darkness,' the tyranny and dominion of Satan, sin, and ignorance, and 'translated,' transferred and carried over as a conquered people removed to a new realm, into 'the kingdom of his dear Son,' the gracious reign of the beloved Christ, so the believer has been moved out of one dominion into another, from death to life, from Satan to Christ).

themes: who hath delivered us from the power of darkness
hath translated us into the kingdom of his dear Son
rescued from Satan's tyranny
carried into the Son's gracious reign

schools.summary: Gill: redemption is an excellent and wonderful blessing of grace which the saints have in and by Christ, lying in a deliverance from sin, all sin, original and actual, under which they are held captive in a state of nature and by which they are made subject to the punishment of death; but through the sacrifice of Christ it is taken and put away, finished, and made an end of, and they are freed; Paul declaring that in Christ they have redemption through his blood, even the forgiveness of sins — the redemption and pardon purchased by Christ's blood (Paul naming the ground of the believer's place in the Son's kingdom: in Christ they have 'redemption,' a being bought back and ransomed from sin's captivity and condemnation, and that 'through his blood,' the price paid being the precious blood of Christ shed in his sacrifice, and explaining this redemption as 'the forgiveness of sins,' the full and free pardon of all their transgressions, so the deliverance rests upon the atoning blood).

themes: in whom we have redemption through his blood
even the forgiveness of sins
the believer ransomed from sin's captivity
the deliverance resting on the atoning blood

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul, discoursing of the dignity of the Son, says 'who is the image of the invisible God,' and asks whose image you would have him be — God's; then he is exactly like the One to whom you assign him as image (the image of the invisible must itself be invisible and equal); Paul declaring that Christ is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of every creature — the deity and supremacy of the Son over all creation (Paul opening his great hymn with two weighty titles: 'the image of the invisible God,' the perfect, exact representation and manifestation of the unseen God, himself sharing the divine nature, so that to see him is to see the Father, and 'the firstborn of every creature'); the debate on the latter title: whether, as the Arians urged, it makes Christ a creature, the first thing made, or whether, as the orthodox hold, 'firstborn' denotes not origin in time but priority, dignity, and sovereign right over all creation, Christ being before all and Lord of all, the heir and head of the whole created order.

debates.question: Does 'the firstborn of every creature' make Christ a creature?

sides.position: No — 'firstborn' (prototokos) denotes not origin in time but priority, dignity, and sovereign right over all creation; Christ is before all and Lord of all, the heir and head, the image of the invisible God sharing the divine nature
The Arians urged it makes Christ the first and highest creature, brought into being before the rest

themes: who is the image of the invisible God
the firstborn of every creature
the deity and supremacy of the Son
the exact image, with priority over all creation

schools.summary: Gill: this proves Christ to be before all creatures, their common Parent, and to have government over them, since he is the Creator of them; the creation by him is not to be understood of the new creation (for whenever that is spoken of the word 'new' is generally used or something equivalent), but of the original creation of all things; Paul declaring that by Christ all things were created, in heaven and earth, visible and invisible, thrones, dominions, principalities, powers — all things created by him and for him — the universal creatorship of Christ proving his deity (Paul grounding Christ's preeminence over creation in the fact that he made it all: every created thing, in the heavens and on the earth, the seen and the unseen, including the very highest ranks of the angelic world, owes its being to Christ, created not only 'by him' as the agent and maker but 'for him' as the end, owner, and goal of all, so the One who could be no creature is the Creator and Lord of all).

debates.question: What does Christ's creating 'all things... by him, and for him' prove?

sides.position: His true deity — the maker of all (heaven and earth, visible and invisible, even the angelic ranks) is no creature but the Creator, owner, and goal of all

themes: for by him were all things created
thrones, or dominions, or principalities, or powers
all things were created by him, and for him
Christ the Creator of all, proving his deity

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is before all things — not only in dignity (being preferable to angels and men in his nature, names, offices, and works, and worthy of more honour than all creatures) but in existence, as he must needs be since they are all made by him; he was before John the Baptist his forerunner, before Abraham who saw his day, before the first man was made, before the angels were in being or the heavens and the earth, being eternal; Paul declaring that Christ is before all things, and by him all things consist — the eternal priority and sustaining power of Christ (Paul pressing Christ's supremacy further: he 'is before all things,' existing before all creation eternally and in dignity above all, and 'by him all things consist,' hold together, are upheld and maintained in their being and order, so the same Christ who created the universe also continually sustains it, the whole frame of things hanging upon him, its eternal Lord and upholder).

themes: and he is before all things
by him all things consist
Christ's eternal priority and sustaining power
the whole frame of things hanging upon him

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the church' is meant not any particular congregated church (as Colosse or Corinth) but the whole election of grace, the general assembly and church of the firstborn whose names are written in heaven in the Lamb's book of life, the church Christ gave himself for, purchased with his blood, builds on himself the rock, and will present to himself; Paul declaring that Christ is the head of the body the church, the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, that in all things he might have the preeminence — the headship and the universal preeminence of Christ (Paul turning from Christ's supremacy over creation to his supremacy over the new creation: 'the head of the body, the church,' its sovereign, source of life, and ruler, 'the beginning,' the origin and first cause of the church's spiritual life, and 'the firstborn from the dead,' the first to rise to immortal life, the head and pledge of his people's resurrection, the whole purpose being that 'in all things,' in both creation and redemption, Christ might hold the first place, the absolute preeminence).

themes: he is the head of the body, the church
the beginning, the firstborn from the dead
that in all things he might have the preeminence
Christ supreme over creation and the church

schools.summary: Gill: 'the Father' is rightly supplied (not in the original text but he is expressly mentioned in the context), since he is the one who makes the saints meet to be partakers of the heavenly glory, delivers them from the power of sin, translates them into the kingdom of his dear Son, and reconciles all things to himself by Christ, and whose sovereign will and pleasure it is; Paul declaring that it pleased the Father that in Christ all fulness should dwell — the indwelling fulness of the Godhead and grace in Christ (Paul giving the ground of all Christ's preeminence: it was the Father's good pleasure, his sovereign will and gracious purpose, that in Christ 'all fulness' should permanently 'dwell,' the whole fulness of the Godhead, his complete deity, and the fulness of grace, truth, and every spiritual blessing for his people, so Christ is the inexhaustible storehouse from which all the church's supply is drawn, and there is no need to seek any supplement in angels or ordinances beside him).

themes: for it pleased the Father
that in him should all fulness dwell
the fulness of the Godhead and grace in Christ
Christ the inexhaustible storehouse

schools.summary: Gill: it was the Father's good pleasure from all eternity, as to lay up all fulness in Christ for his chosen people, so to reconcile them to himself by Christ, which is another reason why Christ is and ought to be considered the head of the church, since their reconciliation is by him; Paul declaring that, having made peace through the blood of his cross, the Father reconciled all things to himself by Christ, whether in earth or in heaven — the peace and reconciliation accomplished by the cross (Paul naming the crowning work of Christ's mediation: by him the Father has 'reconciled all things to himself,' restoring to harmony with God what sin had set at enmity, this peace made 'through the blood of his cross,' the atoning death being the price and ground of all reconciliation, reaching to 'things in earth' and 'things in heaven,' gathering up under one head, in a restored and peaceful order, the whole company of God's redeemed in heaven and on earth).

themes: having made peace through the blood of his cross
by him to reconcile all things unto himself
whether they be things in earth, or things in heaven
the reconciliation accomplished by the cross

schools.summary: Chrysostom: here Paul shows that Christ reconciled those even who were unworthy of reconciliation; by saying they were under the power of darkness he shows the calamity in which they were, but lest, on hearing of 'the power of darkness,' one should consider it mere necessity, he adds 'and you that were alienated,' so that though it appear the same thing, it is not, for it is not the same to deliver out of darkness as to reconcile the willing enemy; Paul declaring that they, once alienated and enemies in mind by wicked works, have now been reconciled — the rich grace shown to former enemies (Paul applying the great reconciliation personally to the Colossians: they had been 'alienated,' estranged and cut off from God and the life of God, and 'enemies in their mind,' hostile to God in understanding and affections, the enmity shown in 'wicked works,' and yet, wonder of grace, this very company of rebels God had 'now reconciled,' bringing his enemies into peace and friendship with himself).

themes: you, that were sometime alienated and enemies in your mind
by wicked works
yet now hath he reconciled
the rich grace shown to former enemies

schools.summary: Gill: the means by which the reconciliation was made was 'in the body of his flesh through death' (or 'through his death,' as some copies and versions read), that is, by the offering up of Christ's body on the cross, his true human body given in sacrifice, which was applied to the Colossians at their conversion whereby their minds were actually reconciled to God; Paul declaring that the reconciliation was wrought in the body of Christ's flesh through death, to present them holy, unblameable, and unreproveable in his sight — the bodily death of Christ and the perfect presentation of his people (Paul naming both means and end: the means 'the body of his flesh through death,' Christ's real human body offered up in his atoning death, against any who denied his true humanity, and the end the final, glorious presentation of his people before God 'holy,' sanctified, 'unblameable,' free from fault, and 'unreproveable,' against whom no charge can stand, so the purpose of the cross reaches all the way to the church's spotless perfection in God's sight).

themes: in the body of his flesh through death
to present you holy and unblameable and unreproveable
Christ's bodily death the means of reconciliation
the spotless presentation of his people the end

schools.summary: Gill: the continuance is in the doctrine of faith which they had received and embraced, in the grace of faith and its exercise implanted in them, and in the profession of faith they had made — not that the virtue and efficacy of Christ's blood, sufferings, and reconciliation depended on their faith and abiding in it, but that faith and continuance in it were necessary means of and evidences of a reconciled state; Paul adding the condition — if they continue in the faith, grounded and settled, not moved from the hope of the gospel they had heard, which was preached to every creature, of which he was made a minister — the necessity of steadfast perseverance in the gospel (Paul, having set forth the glorious presentation of the reconciled, adding that this is theirs 'if' they continue in the faith, 'grounded and settled,' firmly founded like a building on its foundation and steadfast, not shifted from the gospel hope; not that perseverance earns the blessing but that it is the appointed evidence and path of those truly reconciled, the gospel that calls them being the world-wide message of which Paul was a minister).

debates.question: Does 'if ye continue in the faith' make reconciliation depend on perseverance?

sides.position: No — the virtue of Christ's reconciliation does not depend on their faith; perseverance is rather the necessary evidence and appointed path of those truly reconciled

themes: if ye continue in the faith grounded and settled
be not moved away from the hope of the gospel
preached to every creature which is under heaven
perseverance the evidence of the truly reconciled

schools.summary: Gill: Paul, as soon as he mentions his being a minister, speaks of his sufferings, which always attend such an office — appointed to them by God, foretold by Christ, necessary, and to be patiently endured; Paul declaring that he now rejoices in his sufferings for them, filling up what is behind of the afflictions of Christ in his flesh for the church's sake — the joyful sharing in Christ's afflictions for the church (Paul, far from grieving over his imprisonment and trials, rejoicing in them as endured 'for' the Colossians, and speaking of 'filling up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ,' not the atoning sufferings of Christ, which are complete and need no supplement, but the sufferings Christ's members endure for his sake, which Christ counts as his own since the head suffers in the body, the appointed measure of which the apostle bears his share in his own flesh for the good of the church, Christ's body).

debates.question: What is 'filling up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ'?

sides.position: Not Christ's atoning sufferings (which are complete and need no supplement), but the sufferings his members endure for his sake, which Christ counts as his own (the head suffering in the body) — Paul bearing his share for the church

themes: who now rejoice in my sufferings for you
fill up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ
in my flesh for his body's sake, which is the church
the sufferings of Christ's members, not his atoning death

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was made a minister — not of Christ or of the gospel as before (though both were true), but of the churches for whose sake he endured afflictions, which carries a reason of his suffering for them; not a saviour of the body, nor a redeemer, nor Lord of it, but a minister, a servant, who ministered to it in holy things, in the word and ordinances; Paul declaring that he was made a minister of the church according to the dispensation of God given him for them, to fulfil the word of God — the divine stewardship to make the word fully known (Paul describing his ministry as a 'dispensation,' a stewardship, an office of trust, given him by God for the church's sake, the charge being 'to fulfil the word of God,' fully to preach and make known the whole counsel of God, completing and carrying out to the full the message entrusted to him, so his apostolic office was a sacred trust for the benefit of the church, discharged by the full proclamation of God's truth).

themes: whereof I am made a minister
according to the dispensation of God which is given to me for you
to fulfil the word of God
the divine stewardship to make the word fully known

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having shown the lovingkindness of God and the honour by the greatness of the things given, Paul introduces yet another consideration that heightens them — that neither before us did anyone know him, as he does in Ephesians (3:5, 9, 10), neither angels nor principalities nor any other created power but only the Son of God knew; and he said not simply 'hid' but 'quite hid'; Paul speaking of the mystery hidden from ages and generations but now made manifest to his saints — the long-concealed mystery now unveiled to God's people (Paul magnifying the word he was charged to fulfil as a 'mystery': a truth, the gospel and chiefly the Gentiles' full inclusion in Christ, which had been 'hid from ages and from generations,' kept secret through all the long course of time past, unknown even to the angelic world, but which is 'now made manifest to his saints,' openly unveiled in the gospel age to the people of God, so what was once a deep secret is now the open possession of believers).

themes: the mystery which hath been hid from ages and from generations
but now is made manifest to his saints
the long-concealed mystery now unveiled
once a deep secret, now the open possession of believers

schools.summary: Gill: the spring and cause of the manifestation of the gospel to the saints and chosen of God is not their works (for God does not call them with a holy calling according to them but according to his own grace), nor any preparations or dispositions in them (there being none such naturally), nor a foresight of their better improvement, but God's own sovereign grace and will; Paul declaring that God would make known the riches of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles, which is Christ in them, the hope of glory — the indwelling Christ as the hope of glory (Paul naming the very heart and treasure of the mystery: the 'riches of the glory' of it, displayed especially 'among the Gentiles,' that they too should share in Christ, and summing it up in a phrase of unmatched preciousness, 'Christ in you, the hope of glory,' Christ himself dwelling in the believer's heart by his Spirit and faith, the ground, pledge, and sure hope of the coming glory, so the indwelling Christ is at once the believer's present treasure and his guarantee of heaven).

themes: the riches of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles
which is Christ in you, the hope of glory
the indwelling Christ the believer's treasure
Christ the ground and pledge of coming glory

schools.summary: Gill: Christ — the riches, glory, and mystery of the gospel, the hope set before lost sinners to lay hold upon, the only Saviour and Redeemer by whose righteousness believers are justified, through whose blood their sins are pardoned, by whose sacrifice atonement is made, and in whose person alone is acceptance with God, Christ and him crucified and salvation by him — was the subject Paul preached; Paul declaring that they preach Christ, warning and teaching every man in all wisdom, that they may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus — the universal aim of the ministry to perfect every man in Christ (Paul summing up his ministry: its one subject Christ, 'whom we preach,' its method faithful 'warning,' admonishing of sin and danger, and 'teaching,' instructing in the truth, and that 'in all wisdom,' with all prudence and the whole counsel of God, and its goal universal and complete, 'to present every man perfect in Christ Jesus,' that every soul under his care might be brought to maturity and completeness in Christ, lacking nothing).

themes: whom we preach, warning every man, and teaching every man
in all wisdom
that we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus
the universal aim to perfect every man in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Paul laboured in the word and doctrine, by preaching Christ, warning sinners of their danger, teaching the way of salvation and their duty, with the view that in the great day of account he might bring a large number and set them before Christ as the seals of his ministry, instances of the grace of Christ, and perfect in him; Barnes: 'striving' is literally 'agonizing'; Paul declaring that for this end he labours, striving according to the working of Christ which works mightily in him — the toil of the apostle empowered by Christ's might (Paul, having named the goal of presenting every man perfect, owning the cost: he 'labours,' toils to weariness, and 'strives,' agonizes as in a contest with intense effort, and yet not in his own strength but 'according to his working, which worketh in me mightily,' by the mighty energy of Christ working powerfully within him, so all his strenuous effort is itself the fruit and operation of the indwelling power of Christ).

themes: whereunto I also labour, striving
according to his working, which worketh in me mightily
the toil of the apostle empowered by Christ's might
strenuous effort the fruit of indwelling power
